summary: Jeff Atwood documents his upgrade to a plasma television for use with a home theater PC, discovering that HDTV/EDTV displays work remarkably well as computer monitors compared to analog sets. After connecting his PC via VGA and achieving pixel-sharp output at 852x480, he notices an unexpected consequence: standard DVDs now look noticeably blurry compared to high-definition Windows Media content. This leads him to question whether DVD quality, encoded in aging MPEG2, is becoming obsolete in the face of higher resolution displays.
key_points: HDTV and EDTV displays make far superior computer monitors than analog televisions, supporting true resolutions from 850x480 up to 1024x768 or higher
Plasma TVs with VGA ports can connect directly to PCs, with nVidia drivers natively supporting HDTV resolutions and custom timing
High-definition Windows Media content reveals just how limited DVD quality actually is
DVD's MPEG2 encoding is showing its age, with even modest EDTV resolution exceeding what DVDs can deliver
money_quotes: HDTV or EDTV sets fare much better as computer displays.
My basic EDTV set produces an amazing pixel-sharp HTPC image at its maximum resolution of 852x480.
MPEG2 is starting to show its age.
Even 852x480 is more resolution than you need for DVD playback.
key_insight: The transition to higher-resolution displays exposes the limitations of existing media formats, suggesting DVD quality may soon feel as outdated as VHS.
